title: Relevant Precedents and Institutional Pathways
subtitle: What comparable university models suggest for SignWriting at Iowa
summary: A University of Iowa-facing supporting presentation using language, archive, sign-language, and data-infrastructure precedents to show that SignWriting follows a recognizable institutional path rather than requiring a speculative leap.
synopsis: The SignWriting proposal fits a familiar institutional pattern. In multiple cases, a technically mature language or data resource already existed before a university provided a credible home, practical shared capacity, and grant leverage that allowed the effort to scale into durable infrastructure. This deck is meant to support the main Iowa materials by showing that the path is recognizable, not by restating the whole Iowa pitch on its own.

slides.title: Why Precedents Matter
slides.summary: The SignWriting proposal becomes easier to understand when it is framed as a familiar institutional pattern rather than as a one-off special case.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why precedents matter.
slides.speech: These precedents do not need to be exact matches. What matters is that the path itself is recognizable. Mature assets exist first, a university provides a credible home, practical capacity grows around that home, and durable infrastructure follows.
slides.notes: Keep the framing grounded in pattern recognition rather than overclaiming.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Existing Asset Base] --> B[Institutional Home]
  B --> C[Shared Technical Capacity]
  C --> D[Grant and Funding Leverage]
  D --> E[Durable Infrastructure]

slides.title: The Repeatable Pattern
slides.summary: Across language and data infrastructure, the same sequence appears repeatedly: existing assets, credible housing, practical support, then durable growth.
slides.speech: None of these efforts began by inventing a full institution from nothing. They began because something valuable already existed and needed a stronger home, clearer stewardship, and the ability to grow credibly.
slides.notes: This is the framing slide that helps every later example read as a structural analog.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Already-Real Resource] --> B[Credible Home]
  B --> C[Operational Capacity]
  C --> D[Funding and Collaboration]
  D --> E[Durable Shared Resource]

slides.title: ICPSR at Michigan
slides.summary: ICPSR shows how a university can take an already-real data-sharing need and turn it into enduring shared research infrastructure.
bullets: Long-term preservation, curation, and researcher services
slides.speech: ICPSR is one of the clearest precedents for the data-infrastructure lane. Important datasets already existed, but the university home made curation, documentation, preservation, and researcher services durable and scalable.
slides.notes: Emphasize that ICPSR did not create the need. It created the structure that allowed the need to be served well.

slides.title: ELDP and ELAR
slides.summary: ELDP and ELAR show how philanthropy can launch language infrastructure effectively when paired with real institutional hosting and archiving obligations.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for ELDP and ELAR precedent.
slides.speech: This example matters because it shows philanthropy working best when it reinforces an institutional model rather than replacing one. The funding helped launch a durable documentation and archive system because the effort had a real organizational home.
slides.notes: This is the philanthropy analog. Connect it directly to Iowa's complementary-donor lane.

slides.title: PARADISEC
slides.summary: PARADISEC shows that distributed university support can still create durable preservation infrastructure when stewardship is taken seriously.
slides.speech: PARADISEC is useful because it demonstrates that the institutional home does not have to look like a single large center on day one. Shared responsibility, preservation, and access can still become durable when the structure is real.
slides.notes: This is the distributed-support analog. Useful if Iowa conversations broaden beyond one lab.

slides.title: BSL Corpus to BSL SignBank
slides.summary: UCL provides one of the closest sign-language precedents: corpus work became a reusable lexical and research resource under university stewardship.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for BSL SignBank precedent.
slides.speech: This is one of the most important analogs because it is not just a language archive. It is sign-language infrastructure becoming a durable public resource used across research, teaching, and community contexts.
slides.notes: Say clearly that this is one of the closest sign-language-specific examples.

slides.title: ASLLRP and ASL SignBank
slides.summary: Boston University's ASL infrastructure shows how sign-language research ecosystems often converge around corpora, lexical tools, annotations, and technical stewardship.
slides.speech: This is not the same model as SignWriting, but it reinforces a key point: sign-language infrastructure naturally grows into corpora, lexical databases, tools, and public research resources that need ongoing stewardship.
slides.notes: Treat this as adjacent but strengthening the sign-language argument.

slides.title: MIDAS as the Immediate-Capacity Analog
slides.summary: MIDAS shows how shared technical capacity, proposal support, and pilot funding can be built into a university structure before a broader institute-scale identity fully matures.
slides.speech: MIDAS is especially useful for the immediate Iowa step. It models the idea that shared technical capacity can be institutionally valuable right away, while also generating the collaborations and proposal leverage that support longer-term infrastructure growth.
slides.notes: This is the clearest analog for the Steve-first, capacity-first phase.

slides.title: What These Examples Clarify
slides.summary: These examples clarify the structure of the Iowa path: existing assets first, credible institutional housing next, then practical capacity and longer-term growth.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what the precedents clarify.
slides.speech: The precedents clarify a pattern. Valuable assets often exist before the university structure is fully in place. What the institution adds is credibility, housing, staffing, and the ability to grow the work responsibly.
slides.notes: Keep this on pattern recognition rather than turning it into a full ask slide.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Existing Asset Base] --> B[Credible Home]
  B --> C[Practical Capacity]
  C --> D[Long-Term Growth]

slides.title: How This Deck Should Be Used
slides.summary: This deck works best as support material when a reader needs outside analogs, not as the first or only Iowa-facing presentation.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for how to use the precedents deck.
bullets: Use after the main Iowa framing is understood
Use when someone asks for comparable models
Use to reinforce legitimacy, not replace the main pitch
slides.speech: This deck is best used when someone asks whether there is a real model for this kind of move. It should reinforce the main Iowa materials, not substitute for them.

slides.title: Why This Matters for Iowa
slides.summary: These precedents position Iowa not as the inventor of SignWriting's value, but as the institution that can provide its next credible home.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why this matters for Iowa.
slides.speech: The core implication for Iowa is straightforward. The university is not being asked to gamble on a speculative idea. It is being asked to provide the kind of credible home that has helped comparable language and data infrastructure efforts become durable and fundable.

slides.title: Bottom Line
slides.summary: The path being proposed for SignWriting is recognizable; what remains unusual is that the asset base already exists while the university home still does not.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for bottom line of precedent case.
bullets: Existing technical and community asset base
Iowa as the next credible home
slides.speech: The bottom line is simple. SignWriting does not need a university to invent its value. It needs a university to recognize, house, and scale value that already exists. These precedents show that this is a familiar institutional move, not a speculative leap.
slides.notes: End with confidence and clarity. The point is legitimacy, not novelty.
